data.title: IAMGOLD Corporation: Sparkling, But Not My Nugget Of Choice
data.description:
IAMGOLD Corporation (NYSE:IAG) has experienced a stellar performance over the last year, but with many gold mining stocks to choose from, it is not my preferred pick in the sector. I will explain why I favour Harmony Gold (NYSE:HMY) and Pan African Resources (OTCQX:PAFRY) at the moment, in particular.

Gold Above $3,000

With nerves fraying, investors have sought comfort in gold's timeless embrace. I must admit that it has surprised me somewhat to see the gold price pull back a bit after President Trump's announcement of "Liberation Day" tariffs (although gold is still trading comfortably above the $3,000 mark) - but I attributed a portion of this move to panic selling, and investors liquidating positions to meet margin calls, as well as a likely rotation into alternative assets. Notably, markets were off to the races on the 9th of April when President Trump announced that there would be a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs for countries that did not retaliate against the U.S. - but I still hold the view that we will remain in a period of heightened geopolitical tension, which tends to support the gold price.

Central Banks have continued buying gold in large quantities. The World Gold Council reported that official gold reserves of Central Banks around the world increased by another 24 tonnes in February, alone. It is my view that in the wake of President Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs, many central banks around the world, especially those that are hostile towards the United States, like some members in the BRICS bloc are, will continue selling Dollar assets (including US treasuries), and replace a portion of those assets with gold . Notably, China is the second-largest foreign holder of US treasuries (after Japan) - and may potentially exploit this leverage in its current trade war with the United States - by selling large quantities of treasuries in a short period of time, driving up bond yields.

Business Overview And 2024 Results By Operation

IAMGOLD Corporation is a Canadian based gold miner with three operating mines, including two in Canada and one in Burkina Faso.

Côté Gold (Canada)

The Côté Gold mine, based in Ontario, Canada, contributed 18.59% towards IAG's 2024 attributable gold production. See a virtual tour of the mine here. The mine started producing gold at the end of March 2024, and is currently ramping up to full capacity, which it expects to reach in Q4'2025. The average mined ore grade of 1.07 g/t in the last quarter of 2024 was in line with the mine plan. AISC's for Q4 were US$1,685 per ounce sold, and US$1,569 per ounce if excluding non-recurring costs related to construction activities at the tailings dam that will support the life-of-mine.

Côté ran into some operational challenges, which management described as typical for a new mine during the ramp-up phase and not a cause for concern.

In the Q4'2024 Report, management remarked that:

The target is to achieve the steady-state nameplate throughput rate of 36,000 tpd in the fourth quarter of 2025. In December, the plant operated at an average of 87% of the designed throughput level over a two-week period, prior to an unscheduled shutdown due to a split in a conveyor belt associated with ramp-up wear and tear. Repairs were made to the belts and replacement with a modified design was completed in January 2025. When online in December, the plant averaged 1,593 tph, or effectively 106% of nameplate, with a record daily throughput of 42,635 tonnes per day. Subsequent to quarter end, the HPGR rollers demonstrated accelerated wear necessitating a changeover ahead of schedule and limiting the secondary crushing capacity in January. The changeover of the HPGR rolls was completed in February 2025 with operating and maintenance procedures adjusted to maximize lifespan and optimize future changeover windows. Inside the plant, the grinding circuit was also impacted early in the quarter, due to repairs required on one of the Vertimills following a faulty start-up post-maintenance. Prevention and mitigation procedures have been put in place. Plant throughput is expected to be lower in the first quarter as a result of the impact of the timing of these maintenance issues, while operating guidance has been maintained.

2025 Production is expected to increase by between 81% and 101% at an AISC that is forecasted to be between 11% and 20% lower than that for the fourth quarter (which would make Côté the asset with the lowest AISC in the group).

Westwood Complex (Canada)

The Westwood Complex, located in Québec, Canada, saw exceptional operational performance as it delivered record annual gold production that was 3.07% above the top end of guidance, contributing 20.09% towards IAMGOLD's total attributable gold production. AISC's for Q4 were US$1,688.

2025 production is expected to be similar to 2024 - with management guiding that it will be within a range of 7% lower and 4% higher, and at an AISC that is forecasted to be between US$1,675/oz and US$1,825/oz (compared to US$1,702/oz for the 2024 financial year).

Essakane Mine (Burkina Faso)

IAMGOLD owns 90% of Essakane, with the Burkina Faso government owning the remaining 10% stake. Essakane delivered a 10% increase in attributable gold production for the full year, and contributed 61.32% towards IAMGOLD's total attributable production. 2024 AISC's were US$1,625/oz.

Management has guided that 2025 attributable production is expected to be between 2% and 12% lower, and that AISC's are expected to be in a range of between US$1,675/oz and US$1,825/oz.

In the Q4'2024 Report, management remarked that:

The cost guidance for 2025 is higher than the 2024 cost guidance ranges due to lower production and higher local spending including regional security expenditures, increased community programs, permit fees and taxes. A decrease in capitalized waste mining resulted in less waste stripping costs being capitalized and therefore in a higher proportion of costs included in cash costs with an equal reduction in capitalized waste stripping costs included in AISC.

IAMGOLD's 2025 Full-Year Guidance

Management has guided that 2025 consolidated production is expected to be between 33% and 44% higher, building on the 43% year-on-year growth achieved in 2024. AISC's in 2025 are forecasted to be within a range of US$1,625/oz to US$1,800/oz.

Risks

The most pertinent risk to IAMGOLD - aside from a substantial decline in the gold price - is related to political and security risk.

From the Q4'2024 Results:

The political and security environments in Burkina Faso and its neighbouring countries is distressed and volatility remains elevated, including in the Sahel region where the Company's Essakane mine is located. Burkina Faso and other neighbouring countries in the region experienced military coups in the past couple of years. Stability in the West African region, generally, has been upended by military coups and led to near-total suspension of military assistance from France and the U.S. and exclusion of military governments in joint military task force with other West African countries formed to prevent militant attacks and threats from further spreading in the rest of the region. Given Essakane's significant contribution to the financial condition of the Company, any terrorist attack, change in legislation or ownership, problematic or adverse condition affecting mining, processing, infrastructure, equipment, labour, the supply chain, taxation, legal or reputational status could have a material adverse effect on the Company's business, operations, liquidity and capital resources.

Considering that the operations in Burkina Faso contributed close to two-thirds of IAMGOLD's total attributable production in 2024, I am of the opinion that IAG should be trading at a lower Forward EV/EBITDA ratio than peers with a greater share of their operations in safer jurisdictions.

Valuation

I prefer using Forward EV/EBITDA ratios to value companies in the materials sector, as it guides me in understanding what I am paying for the underlying business versus the earnings that it is forecasted to generate.

In selecting the peer group for my analysis, I have chosen gold mining companies with a market cap larger than $1 billion, and excluded companies whose business models primarily rely on gold royalty and streaming contracts, as opposed to actually owning and operating physical gold mines.

Over the last five years, IAG has traded at an average FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that was 36.53% below that of its peers. IAG is now trading at a FWD EV/EBITDA ratio that is 36.12% below its peers - suggesting that it is fairly valued on a relative basis versus the peer group.

Would I Invest In IAG Now?

Although IAMGOLD delivered an impressive 61% return over the past year and has guided for a significant production increase in 2025 as operations at the Côté Gold mine ramp up, it is not my preferred gold miner.

Emerging market currencies, including the South African Rand, have come under pressure amid renewed risk-off sentiment following the recent market turmoil triggered by President Trump's "Reciprocal" tariffs. In addition, South Africa's newly formed Government of National Unity remains deadlocked over the national budget, adding further strain to the Rand (which has sold off 8.68% against the US Dollar over the last month).

For this reason, I currently see more value in gold mines that have a large proportion of their costs denominated in Rand, with stable production, while also operating at lower AISC's that provide some margin of safety should the gold price retreat.

Harmony Gold (NYSE:HMY), and Pan African Resources (OTCQX:PAFRY) in particular, look compelling to me at present.

data.title: March CPI: Cold Inflation Update Could Allow The Fed To Cut In May
data.description:
In the middle of an uncertain macro environment dominated by headlines about tariffs, the March CPI report provided a more optimistic view on the inflation situation. Soft monthly increases in headline and core inflation driven by declines in several key segments, including energy, may provide the Fed with enough room to cut rates in May if they so choose.

Headline Inflation

For the first time since the pandemic, U.S. CPI saw a monthly decline, coming in at -0.1% MoM in March. With the negative monthly print, the annual rate slowed to 2.4% YoY, the lowest since September 2024, and well below expectations of a slowdown to 2.6% YoY (from 2.8% YoY in February). The negative headline monthly rate was largely a result of energy prices which saw a strong decline in March.

  • The energy CPI component fell -2.4% MoM and -3.3% YoY, down from the -0.2% YoY increase in February. The monthly decline in energy was the largest since May 2023. The drop in gas prices in March drove the decline as that index fell -6.3% MoM (largest decline since December 2022). Its large weight offset rises in the natural gas index (+3.6% MoM) and electricity (+0.9% MoM).
  • Food prices continued their uptrend, posting a moderately strong increase of 0.4% MoM and pushing the annual rate up to 3.0% YoY which is the largest annual increase since October 2023. Food at home prices were up 0.5% MoM with meats up 1.1% MoM and dairy up 1.0% MoM. Food away from home increased 0.4% MoM with prices for full service meals up 0.6% MoM. The trend in food prices which is typically categorized as volatile remains in a steady uptrend. Tariffs are only going to accelerate the trend.

Core Inflation

While the decline in energy prices played a major role in the decline in the headline index, the core inflation segment did come in softer than expected at 0.1% MoM, a monthly rate not seen since May and June of 2024. That soft print helped push core CPI inflation below the three percent threshold to 2.8% YoY in March, the lowest in four years. The disinflation was a result of cool trends in both goods and services that pointed to a broad decline in prices.

  • The core goods segment declined -0.1% MoM in March, the first decline since August 2024. A major subsegment driving the decline was a -0.2% MoM move in transportation commodities that featured a -0.7% MoM decline in used vehicle prices. This offset a slight 0.1% MoM rise in new vehicle prices. There were declines in two other goods segments, medical goods (-1.1% MoM) and recreation goods (-0.3% MoM), and a flat MoM reading in household furnishings & supplies.
  • The core services segment was only up 0.1% MoM, the first increase of that size since August 2021. Another sizeable decline in transportation services played a major role in the soft core print. It fell -1.4% MoM in March, the largest MoM decline since May 2020, after an -0.8% MoM decline in February. Within that index, motor vehicle insurance prices fell -0.8% MoM (largest decline since October 2021) and airfares fell -5.3% MoM (largest decline since September 2021).

  • The shelter CPI component increased just 0.2% MoM, the slowest since June 2024, and the annual increase cooled to 4.0% YoY, the lowest since November 2021. The cool reading is a bit deceptive. The subindex was affected by a -3.5% MoM decline in lodging away from home, the largest drop in over three years, while the rent of primary index increased 0.3% MoM and owners’ equivalent rent was up 0.4% MoM.
  • If we remove the shelter and used car moves from the core CPI reading, prices were unchanged in the month of March for the first time since May 2024. On an annual basis, this special aggregate decelerated from 2.2% YoY in February to 1.8% YoY in March, the lowest in four years.

The soft March CPI print is evidence that weaker discretionary spending is flowing through into prices. Food and housing prices looked like the hottest segments this month while there were declines in discretionary segments like recreation goods and airfare and notable soft prints in recreation services (+0.1% MoM) and household furnishings (0.0% MoM). This is the kind of report that the Fed was looking for in its fight against inflation as it is evidence of demand-driven disinflation. Instead, over the last two years there was a steady strength in the economy that kept inflation sticky.

There is a major caveat to this report in that it is the last CPI reading before the major reciprocal tariffs went into effect and probably too early of a reading to see any impact from the tariffs placed on Mexican and Canadian imports. The impact from the North American tariffs was always going to be limited because most of these imports from the U.S.’s neighbors are for intermediate goods like energy, lumber, and machinery. Autos is the main exception here and new vehicle prices did increase 0.1% MoM, the largest so far in 2025.

Market Reaction

The market reaction to the soft CPI print was interesting. At first, there was a strong decline in Treasury rates as an immediate response to the report. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell about -8 bps and the 10-year yield (US10Y) moved lower by about -4 bps. That move started to unwind about 10 minutes after the release. In fact, by about 8:50 am, the 10-year yield was back to where it was before the CPI report. Similarly, there was a sharp rise in S&P futures (SPX) when the report was released that was reversed in the next 15 minutes. In general, I think that the market recognizes that this is a dovish report, but the current market dynamics dominated by trade headlines have overwhelmed that sentiment.

What the market, especially the bond market, will want to see is how the Fed will shift its rhetoric in response to this report. The cool reading can provide FOMC members with an opportunity to become softer on inflation even with tariffs looming because it is evidence that prices were impacted by demand-driven factors at the end of the first quarter. Because of this, the Fed is also likely to see more downside risks to growth that are unrelated to tariffs, which could push against its narrative that the U.S. economy is clearly strong.

I believe that the Fed could use the soft March inflation report as a reason to cut rates in May. There is solid evidence of weaker demand-side pressure on prices, and this will also show in the next PCE price index update. Additionally, the Fed will be looking at volatility in the bond market which is significantly tightening credit conditions, and it will want to cut to help assuage some market anxiety.

Despite all of that, the Fed will have to balance this with how it sees tariffs impacting inflation even if there is a 90-day pause. The macroenvironment is still very uncertain, and almost impossible for the Fed to navigate flawlessly, but soft inflation in March is definitely good news for the monetary policymakers.

data.title: Investors Title Company: Exposure To The U.S. Housing Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Investors Title Company (NASDAQ:ITIC) is a property title insurance company, one of the lowest risk ventures within the insurance space. Losses and loss provisions on these contracts are very low, and the majority of the company’s expenses are related to paying the commissions to its sales agents.

The company’s financial results are very dependent on the transaction volumes on the real estate markets, and that’s why the dividend payments can fluctuate by quite a wide margin. ITIC’s share price has been strong in the past few years but recently lost some ground when the market started its correction, and as I still expect interest rates to decrease (which should increase the appetite for home buyers), I wanted to keep my fingers on the pulse here.

A profit increase thanks to higher premiums

Investors Title Company’s profit is closely correlated to the transaction volumes on the real estate markets. As the real estate markets gained momentum, ITIC reported a $204M revenue from written premiums, an increase of approximately 20% from the $171M in premiums it generated in 2023. All other revenue items remained relatively stable, which allowed ITIC to report a full-year revenue of $258M, an increase of almost $34M compared to the preceding year.

The operating expenses also increased, mainly on the back of a higher commission paid to agents, which increased by about $24M. That being said, the expenses related to ITIC’s own staff decreased by in excess of $4M, and this absorbed a portion of the impact from higher fees. The total amount of operating expenses increased by $20M, which means the pre-tax income jumped by approximately 50% to $39.5M.

After paying the relevant corporate taxes, the net profit for the year was $31.1M, resulting in an EPS of $16.43 based on the current share count of just under 1.9M shares outstanding.

At the end of last year, ITIC’s book value was just under $252M, with approximately $237M in tangible assets. Divided over the 1.886M shares that are currently outstanding, this results in a tangible book value of $125.5 per share. That’s an increase of approximately $1 per share compared to the $124.5 per share at the end of last year. That’s a pretty small increase, but that’s mainly because ITIC prefers to pay out the vast majority of its earnings in dividends in combination with a small share buyback of around $1M per year.

During 2024, the company paid just under $30M in dividends. The normal quarterly dividend is $0.46 per share, but the company aims to pay out a special dividend that fluctuates with the bottom-line result. In 2022 and 2023, the special dividends were just $3 and $4 per share respectively but during FY 2024, ITIC paid a special dividend of $14/share which brought the full-year dividend to $15.82 per share. This represents a dividend yield of just over 7% based on the current share price. But of course, it goes without saying these dividends will continue to fluctuate depending on ITIC’s financial performance and investors shouldn’t bank on getting fat dividend cheques every year.

Investment thesis

I currently have a small long position in Investors Title Company, but I haven’t added to that position. I was hoping the recent correction would have pushed the ITIC share price lower, but the stock has been quite resilient. I will keep an eye out for an opportunity to add to this position.

data.title: Alibaba: A TikTok Deal Casualty (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

2025 has so far been a fabulous year for Alibaba (NYSE:BABA).

China’s leading e-commerce player finally pulled itself out of its multi-year bottom with a raft of updates on many AI fronts, launching LLMs that beat peers and notched top performances across most LLM benchmarks. I have been quite bullish on Alibaba through last year given its innovation efforts and the promising overhaul in the company’s management a couple of years ago.

I previously expected tariff headwinds to impose upon Alibaba and many other China stocks, as noted in a previous analysis on the company (see: Risks section), but believed that Chinese stocks were in a better place this time around.

Unfortunately, there seems to be a recurring theme that constantly appears to manifest during many press conferences and discussions regarding the current US administration’s strategy around tariffs — that China is unwilling to cooperate on negotiating a deal. More importantly, China is unwilling to negotiate specifically on a deal regarding TikTok, a deal that appears to be in the Trump 2.0 administration’s deepest interests.

And until I can gain some visibility on deal talk advancing around the TikTok deal, I believe Alibaba’s stock will see headwinds through the next quarter or two, leading me to downgrade Alibaba to a Hold.

How The TikTok Deal Holds Chinese Stocks Hostage

The Trump 2.0 sovereign/business interests in TikTok originated during the pandemic lockdowns in 2020, leading to the Trump 1.0 administration signing the deal to ban TikTok. The ban was eventually removed after a series of events involving the fallout of a late-2020 TikTok-takeover deal culminating in the Biden administration ordering a “broad review of potential security risks from Chinese tech companies.”

Fast-forward to 2024, the Biden administration passed a law in April last year that effectively forces either all Chinese stakeholders, including TikTok parent ByteDance, to divest from TikTok or face an all-out TikTok ban. Per the law, TikTok was given time until January 19 this year but got a last-minute 75-day reprieve after Trump 2.0’s EO. As I write this, TikTok has secured a second 75-day reprieve from Trump 2.0, implying compliance until June 9 of this year.

Unfortunately, the complicated nature of deals involving any kind of TikTok takeover also involves compliance with China’s rules on technology exports. In short, any potential TikTok deal will require approval from the Chinese government. This could possibly be a reason why TikTok’s Chinese stakeholders are slow-walking any efforts to sell/divest their stake in TikTok. This still leaves the current Trump 2.0 administration in a bind as to how to comply with the TikTok divestiture/sale law that was passed twelve months ago.

Most of Trump 2.0’s publicized motivation so far regarding the recent reciprocal country-wide tariff menu has been about rewiring America’s supply chains and onshoring manufacturing. Only Canada, China and potentially the EU moved to retaliate against Trump 2.0’s reciprocal tariff program.

Still, the Trump administration chose to suddenly pause the reciprocal tariffs this week, which is something I fully expected in some ways.

However, Trump singled out China in the recent pause on reciprocal tariffs, citing the country’s insistence on escalation of tariff-slapping by both countries. Remember, at the heart of this all, the TikTok sale/divestiture is still in limbo, threatened by China's constant insistence on securing its compliance for the deal to proceed. Trump recently reiterated that the TikTok deal is “on the table” despite China dragging its feet on the deal.

With no clear visibility about a TikTok deal yet, the increasing odds of a US/China showdown continue to keep rising with the US figuring out how to stay compliant with the TikTok divestiture/sale law. At the time of writing this post, US tariffs on China stand at 125%, while Chinese tariffs on the US stand at 84%.

There will be different bargaining chips thrown at the negotiating table from both sides, which will cause significant volatility while capping potential for expanding valuation premiums on Chinese stocks such as Alibaba. Yesterday, the US Treasury Secretary also hinted at the possibility of Chinese stocks getting delisted from the US. Although I do not believe the US will follow through with that just yet, it still will introduce volatility.

What does all of this have to do with Alibaba?

What this all does is momentarily throw cold water on the expanding valuation premiums of Chinese stocks, including Alibaba, which were getting a nice lift so far.

Alibaba’s Valuation Premium Expansion May Pause For A Bit

Therefore, until the US and China arrive at some form of resolution on the TikTok deal compliance, as well as the trade war between the two countries, I believe Alibaba’s valuation premium expansion will be on hold for a few months.

For now, Alibaba trades at a forward valuation of 11.7x FY25 earnings or 10.3x FY26 earnings. (FY25 ends in March every year.). While FY25 EPS growth is expected to grow 4.4%, FY26 EPS growth is expected to grow 13.5%, so these valuation premiums still look reasonable.

But since I feel China’s stock market PE, as noted in Exhibit B, will remain range-bound, I expect Alibaba as well to remain range-bound until the TikTok deal and US/China tariff turbulence settle. This leads me to downgrade Alibaba to a Hold rating due to falling confidence levels in the company sustaining any expansion in its valuation premium in the current trade climate.

Other Factors To Note

Over the long term, I still believe Alibaba has found itself a solid footing with the strong movements it has been making this year in AI. It started the year by making rapid-fire announcements, launching its Qwen family of LLMs that rivaled the performance of DeepSeek and OpenAI.

Most recently, the Chinese e-commerce giant has dropped its hat into the ‘AI-agent ring’ by launching multimodal models under the Qwen family of LLMs, targeting all its international customers as well.

This does look very promising for Alibaba, but unfortunately for the company, e-commerce and internal logistics is still a majority of its revenue, so the company’s growth in the latter two end markets can be threatened by the brewing uncertainty between the US and China.

If Alibaba’s resilient management can somehow demonstrate a path towards sustainable revenue growth and margin revisions to the upside despite the current trade climate, I will reevaluate my Hold rating.

Alternatively, if there does emerge some clarity in the TikTok deal, I believe the overall sentiment surrounding Alibaba and China stocks may once again look favorable.

Takeaway

At the moment, there does not appear to be any visible deal between the US and China on the sale/divestiture of TikTok. The US law requires compliance on TikTok either being sold or its Chinese investors divesting, while China argues that any such deal requires its approval, creating a deadlock for all China stocks, including Alibaba.

Plus, the unresolved trade disputes between the US and China remain unresolved, which may pressure Alibaba’s e-commerce and international logistics revenue segments.

Therefore, although I find Alibaba’s low-teen valuation premium reasonable, I am downgrading the stock to a Hold for now.

data.title: 5 Rocking Income Funds To Consider In This Market Correction
data.description:
When I first became interested in the stock and bond markets and was early on in my investing journey, I knew little about the details of my investments beyond what my financial advisor at the time explained to me. Most of that “knowledge” revolved around a set of mutual fund products that his investment firm promoted. So, when I received an unexpected inheritance in 2007 during what turned out to be a top in the market, I followed his advice and invested all of my inherited funds into a set of mutual funds, plus one BDC (business development company) that he assured me would provide some diversification and protection against a market crash.

A year later, as I helplessly watched the value of my portfolio decline, I realized that I had made two big mistakes. The first was trusting him to advise me on what to invest my money in without doing my own due diligence beyond the single page fact sheet for each mutual fund. The second mistake was going “all in” at the top of the market. For an excellent overview of what a market top feels like, I highly recommend reading this recent discussion from fellow SA analyst, Jim Sloan. For Jim, this is not his first rodeo, and he has the ability to express in easily understandable language that this latest market correction could indeed be the start of a new and potentially long-lasting bear market.

The market is radically overvalued, and will give us no peace or possibility of long-term gain until that excess valuation is wrung out of the system. It's not just the tariffs, although they certainly don't help and perhaps provided the trigger for what was inevitable anyway.

As a now retired income investor, I view these types of market corrections differently than I did when I was still in the accumulation phase of my investing journey. I am also a classic rock music buff and would like to provide my readers some entertainment value along with my suggestions for further research if you are interested in learning how to be a successful income investor. Each of these classic rock songs carry a message that I will translate into action based on today’s wildly volatile market environment.

You Ain’t Seen Nothing Yet (Bachman-Turner Overdrive)

Here's somethin' that you're never gonna forget

Following the theme of the market top and psychology-driven sentiment that Jim Sloan discusses in his article, it might appear after today’s news that tariffs will be “paused for 90 days” that the worst is now over and everyone can get back to buying the wildly overpriced Mag 7 (MAGS) tech stocks. This simplified version of the Wall Street Cheat Sheet’s Psychology of a Market Cycle chart from Finimize illustrates the 13 emotions that investors typically go through during a boom and bust market cycle. In my opinion, we are currently somewhere between 8, Anxiety and 9, Denial. Notice how the market drifts slightly higher during the Denial stage before Panic sets in and sends prices down further. In fact, we witnessed that type of market action today as the Nasdaq-100 surged 10% in response to the tariff pause.

Because this article is about income investing and how one might want to prepare for the next phase of the market cycle, I am going to suggest one pick for each song that relates to the theme. In this case, my gut feeling tells me that we have not yet seen the worst of the downturn in the market, and therefore a more defensive income pick might be a good choice today. A fixed income CEF (closed end fund) that invests in credit securities (e.g., loans, bonds) offers some protection against market volatility and can deliver consistent income as long as credit markets remain relatively stable. My recommendation for this type of income investment is FS Credit Opportunities (FSCO).

One advantage that FSCO has over other similar funds is their access to private credit markets. As I discussed in my most recent review of FSCO, the fund’s ability to dynamically allocate between public and private credit offers relative safety and strong income generation potential. I have recommended FSCO several times in the past two years, starting in September 2023, and it has delivered strong total returns in that time. With the recent market correction, FSCO returned to a significant discount to NAV after it had mostly closed that discount recently. This chart from CEFConnect illustrates the sudden substantial price drop that resulted in what is now an outstanding opportunity to buy a quality CEF at an excellent price, in my opinion.

Stairway to Heaven (Led Zeppelin)

There's a lady who's sure all that glitters is gold

And she's buying a stairway to Heaven

The idea behind this theme is that when I read something like “just buy stocks because they always go up”, I want to scream. Yes, if you were lucky enough to buy stocks at the start of a boom cycle, and were brave enough to hold on without selling through the bust cycles, and your timeframe is long enough, you can make money. To illustrate that point, just look at any stock chart of the S&P 500 over the past 20 years, 50 years, etc. As an example to illustrate my point, here is a chart from Macrotrends of the past 90 years showing the S&P 500 price movements.

That is the allure of investing in stocks, but it also leads to poor decisions when the effects of market psychology are not properly factored in. As some have famously stated, the market can remain irrational far longer than one can remain solvent. Furthermore, as an income investor, one would need to make consistently good decisions on when to buy stocks and when to sell to capture gains, and the market action may not cooperate if you need to sell to capture the income before the next crash occurs. This is where my Income Compounder strategy comes in. There is no need to sell when prices are down to collect income from a fund that pays a consistent monthly distribution, regardless of the current market price.

For this theme, I wish to highlight the Cornerstone Total Return fund (CRF). When I last reviewed CRF just over a year ago in March 2024, I rated the fund a Buy. The fund holdings nearly mimic those of the S&P 500 and the fund has pretty consistently delivered a total return close to or at times, exceeding that of the SPY. Since that article was published, and with the recent market correction, CRF delivered a total return of 11.5% compared to 4.5% from SPY.

Furthermore, there are two very appealing aspects to the Cornerstone funds, including Cornerstone Strategic Investment Fund (CLM), which is nearly identical in most aspects to CRF. Those two features include: a managed distribution policy that is set at the start of the calendar year and pays a consistent monthly distribution for the next twelve months regardless of what happens in the interim, and a discount to reinvest based on the NAV of the fund regardless of the premium, which often exceeds 20% or more.

Another feature that is often discussed is the opportunity to acquire shares at a substantial discount to the market price during rights offerings. A rights offering or RO was just announced on April 8 for both CLM and CRF that will take effect on April 21. The details of the upcoming RO can be found at this link.

Cornerstone Total Return Fund, Inc. (the “Fund”) is issuing non-transferable rights (“Rights”) to its holders of record of shares of common stock (“Common Stock”) (such holders hereinafter referred to as “Stockholders” and the shares of Common Stock, the “Shares”) which Rights will allow Stockholders to subscribe for new Shares (the “Offering”). For every three (3) Rights a Stockholder receives, such Stockholder will be entitled to buy one (1) new Share. Each Stockholder will receive one Right for each outstanding Share it owns on April 21, 2025 (the “Record Date”). Fractional Shares will not be issued upon the exercise of the Rights. Accordingly, the number of Rights to be issued to a Stockholder on the Record Date will be rounded up to the nearest whole number of Rights evenly divisible by three. Stockholders on the Record Date may purchase Shares not acquired by other Stockholders in this Rights offering, subject to certain limitations discussed in this Prospectus. Additionally, if there are not enough unsubscribed Shares to honor all additional subscription requests, the Fund may, in its sole discretion, issue additional Shares up to 100% of the Shares available in the Offering to honor additional subscription requests.

Once in a Lifetime (Talking Heads)

And you may find yourself living in a shotgun shack

And you may find yourself in another part of the world

And you may find yourself behind the wheel of a large automobile

And you may find yourself in a beautiful house, with a beautiful wife

And you may ask yourself, "Well, how did I get here?"

I think a lot of us are asking ourselves similar questions right now. The reason for picking this song is because we are in the midst of a once-in-a-lifetime extreme market correction, not too dissimilar from what happened in 2020 during the Covid pandemic. For income investors, it may be a unique opportunity to buy high-yielding income vehicles at bargain-basement prices. Then, five years from now, you may find yourself in that beautiful house or behind the wheel of that large EV.

For this income pick, I want to highlight Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC). This CEF is one that I have discussed previously on multiple occasions, most recently in January of this year. With uncertainty around interest rates starting to fall, there was some concern that lower rates might impact the performance of funds that invest in CLOs. What I wrote in that article still holds true today, although there have been several significant developments since then.

OXLC has rewarded investors with high-yield distributions currently yielding 21% and now declared through June. The price of the CEF is lower than the price when insiders made substantial open market purchases last year. The potential exists for future distribution increases based on the massive new warehouse investments in new CLO equity positions, along with the carryover of undistributed taxable income from the previous three fiscal quarters.

The CLO market is booming with substantial new CLO issuances in 2024 with loan default rates remaining relatively low compared to historical averages. If you do not already own shares of OXLC in your income portfolio, I highly recommend buying some shares while the price remains low, regardless of what happens with future interest rates.

Since that article was published, OXLC declared another three months of distributions continuing at $0.09 per share through September. Then at the end of March there was some irrational selling of OXLC shares that led to outstanding buying opportunities for patient, long-term income investors. The price dropped briefly below $4 per share before recovering and now trades at $4.69 as of 4/9/25 resulting in a forward yield of nearly 25% on shares bought at the current price. On March 20, during the height of the paranoia, OXLC announced a $150M share repurchase program.

For investors in a fund like OXLC is important to keep in mind that the price of equities has no correlation to CLOs, which are essentially bundled corporate loans. CLOs tend to outperform other asset classes during times of market distress as long as default rates do not suddenly shoot higher.

Furthermore, OXLC recently acquired substantial new CLO equity investments that have not even started making waterfall payments yet, which add to the income stream. I also discussed that point in my January coverage of the fund.

Starting next quarter, Q4 2025 for the quarter ending March 31 (the end of the OXLC fiscal year), some income from those new investments will begin to pay off, with $151M of CLO equity making their first quarterly waterfall cash distributions. The slide below from the Q3 earnings presentation shows the expected contributions from the new CLO investments and the timing of when those additional sources of income that total $509M will begin to be realized.

Gimme Shelter (Rolling Stones)

Ooh, a storm is threatening

My very life today

If I don't get some shelter

Ooh yeah I'm gonna fade away

Going back to the notion that we may have just witnessed a recent market top and despite today’s relief rally, we may not have gotten the bear out of the woods just yet. This recommendation is for income investors who wish to beat inflation but also have capital preservation as an important consideration. This suggestion is another fund that invests in CLOs, but it is an ETF that invests in the AAA-rated debt tranches of those CLOs. Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA) is a top-rated ETF that offers a yield of just over 6% based on a variable distribution. It was one of the most popular of all actively traded ETFs in 2024 with net inflows of over $7B. I reviewed JAAA in October 2024 and this was my summary at that time.

I believe that JAAA deserves a spot in any income investor’s portfolio due to its strong performance relative to other fixed income investments, and one that also offers capital preservation in times of extended market volatility. From the Q3 2024 fund commentary, the market outlook for AAA-rated CLOs remains positive, with the strength of the US economy continuing to support new CLO issuance. The portfolio managers are well positioned to take advantage of the current market environment, regardless of the pace of rate cuts.

This one-year chart illustrates the defensive nature of JAAA, which has lost only about 1% YTD compared to the SPY which is down -7% (after recovering from a deeper correction).

Money for Nothing (Dire Straits)

That ain't working, that's the way you do it

Money for nothing, and your chicks for free

Investing may not be as glamorous as rock and roll, but it is not really “work” either. At least not as a passive income investor. Warren Buffett once said, “If you don't find a way to make money while you sleep, you will work until you die.” Now that I am no longer “working” (for the man) I enjoy receiving income every month from my investments. That was my goal for retirement, and I am now enjoying my retirement even more knowing that I have a steady income stream coming into my account whether I get out of bed in the morning or not. There is definite freedom knowing that no matter what happens on a day-to-day basis, my income investments will keep on delivering.

For this theme, I wanted to suggest a covered call fund. If you are not already familiar with the notion of income funds that use covered calls, you can read more about them that in my popular review of Covered Call Income Funds for Dummies.

This ETF is based on the performance of the 20-year US Treasury bond, iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond Buywrite Strategy ETF (TLTW). As such, its performance is also uncorrelated to stocks and may, in fact, offer outperformance relative to stocks in an extended bear market. The current environment for long-term Treasury bonds is experiencing some uncertainty along with everything else due to the concerns of a trade war with China, among other things. By writing covered calls against the monthly performance of TLT, the TLTW fund generates substantial high-yield income on a monthly basis that is variable but is currently yielding about 15%.

According to the iShares website for TLTW, there are several additional reasons to consider investing in the ETF.

As a US focused income investor, the long-term stability of Treasuries gives me some comfort in knowing that my income will most likely continue uninterrupted, although it may go up or down depending on external market forces. Bonds have been underperforming stocks over the past few years, so from a total return perspective, some might question the wisdom of investing in this fund now. But past performance is no guarantee of future results, and the times they are a-changing.

Come writers and critics

Who prophesize with your pen

And keep your eyes wide

The chance won't come again

And don't speak too soon

For the wheel's still in spin

And there's no tellin' who

That it's namin'

For the loser now

Will be later to win

For the times they are a-changin'

That’s a wrap. Hope you enjoyed my little musical interlude into your income investing world.

data.title: EWO- Cheap Valuations And Better Growth Prospects Ahead
data.description:
Outperforming the Eurozone And The Globe In 2025

The iShares MSCI Austria ETF (NYSEARCA:EWO), a relatively small and unknown product (it has only accumulated less than $75m of AUM in around three years of listing history) that focuses on 19 stocks from Austria (mainly mid-caps that account for 40% of total holdings) has been one of the better performing geographical ETFs this year. To expand on this, note that while global stocks have lost ground by mid-single-digit percentages (on average), and the Eurozone has notched up gains of less than 10% (on average), EWO has zoomed ahead by 16% on a YTD basis.

If this early high-flyer interests you, here’s some commentary on some of the important variables that impact the fortunes of this product.

What Do the Macros Look Like?

While EWO may be in a good way, the same can’t quite be said about the Austrian economy which has been contracting for a few years now (see image below).

Note however that there have been improvements in the YoY decline over the last 4 quarters, and the Q4 GDP decline of-0.5% was the lowest decline in 7 quarters. Crucially, looking ahead, the Austrian economy is expected to start growing again this year (growth of over 1%), with even better growth prospects in the following year (1.4%).

One of the more encouraging facets to highlight is the potential rebound in consumption spending. In Q4, even though GDP continued to decline, consumer spending picked up quite significantly from the Q3 levels, and was up by around 0.5% YoY coming in around the EUR45.6B mark. For context over the last three decades, the average consumer spending per quarter has only worked out to around EUR40.6B, and the record for any quarter (which was Q3-21) is not too far away at EUR46.4B.

Basically, over the last couple of years, private consumption has been declining, but this year, it will serve as the chief driver of GDP growth, witnessing an expansion of 1.4%, and the improvement should continue even to next year, with expected growth of 1.8%. Consumption is also likely to be supported by the fact that Austrian households don’t really have to be burdened by huge debt loads. As opposed to the post-pandemic years when the debt to income [DTI] levels were at 86%, things are a lot more palatable now with a DTI of only 78% at the end of 2024 (below the long-term average of 82%). The unemployment position too is gradually becoming more palatable, dropping below the 8% levels in March, the first time in three months.

On the business side of the economy, the prospects are still looking quite unsteady with business confidence still in negative territory, and worsening in March from what was seen in February, although one would hope that with the ECB poised to cut rates even further (after cutting rates by 25bps in March, two more further cuts are expected in April and June), business confidence will gradually pick up.

However, we’d still like to highlight the ongoing improvement in industrial production, which had been on a downward YoY slump for well over a year. In the initial months of 2025, industrial production has started growing by around 2% back-to-back.

What Do The Valuations and Income Picture Look Like?

Despite its rise this year, EWO may still continue to find quite a few takers because its forward valuations look very compelling. Note that EWO’s holdings are on course to deliver a similar threshold of long-term earnings growth as the Eurozone (around 9-10%), and not too far off from the sub-11% growth expected by global stocks. Yet, EWO can be picked up at a P/E of just 9x, which represents a 37% discount over European stocks, and a massive 50% discount over global stocks. Of the three counters, it is also the only product with an implied PEG (Price-to-earnings-growth ratio) of less than 1x. Both EZU and VT, have a steeper implied PEG of 1.43x-1.69x.

What enhances EWO’s prospects even further is its stellar yield of almost 7% (EWO’s dividends are paid semi-annually), which is 2.4-3x better than the globe and the Eurozone.

What Do The Charts Suggest?

If you’re looking for suitable pockets that could benefit from upward mean-reversion momentum across the globe, EWO may serve as a potential proxy. Its current relative strength [RS] ratio of less than 0.22x is 29% lower than its long-term average, offering some scope of normalization.

Switching to EWO’s own weekly price imprints, we can see that since over the last 3 years, the ETF has been gaining traction within a certain ascending channel. At current levels of around $24, and considering the boundaries of its channel, we view the risk-reward for a long position as relatively fair.

Amidst the significant sell-off witnessed by global markets in recent days, we’ve also been quite enthused to note that EWO continues to defend its landmark 200DMA on the daily charts, since February. Earlier this week, there were signs that it was going to fall well below this point, but it managed to hold on, and showed good fire-fighting capabilities around the 200DMA, with a potential recovery in sight.

To conclude, we reiterate our long-standing buy rating on EWO.

data.title: KBWD: Poor Dividend History And Declining NAV
data.description:
Overview

Invesco KBW High Dividend Yield Financial ETF (NASDAQ:KBWD) is an ETF made up of companies that operate in the financial sector that offer competitive dividend yields. The ETF is made up of constituents of the KBW Nasdaq Financial Sector Dividend Yield Index, and KBWD has an inception dating back to December of 2020. This means we have over a decade's worth of performance data that we can reference. With interest rates remaining high, I wanted to take a look at this financial-focused ETF to assess how attractive it may be in the current environment.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that KBWD's price has declined by about 17.3%, with most of that decline taking place over the last few weeks alongside the rest of the market. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return is still at a loss of 6.7%, due to the severe price drop. One of the main appeals of KBWD is the generous starting dividend yield of 13.8% and distributions are paid out on a monthly basis. This could make the fund an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high yielding income. However, I remain cautious around the fund's long term track record of price declines.

Despite the fund's challenged past performance, I wanted to assess KBWD to see if entry here would be beneficial to long term investors. However, there are some internal weaknesses that investors should be aware of. For instance, the fund hasn't been able to generate enough earnings to cover the distribution since 2021. Additionally, the fund has exposure to some holdings that I would consider to be less desirable. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategies and holdings that KBWD utilizes in an effort to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

KBWD currently has assets under management of $368M, which is spread across a portfolio of different financial investments. According to the most recent fact sheet, Asset Management & Custody Banks make up the largest portion of the portfolio at 44.03%. This is followed by a sizeable exposure to Mortgage REITs and Regional Banks, accounting for 43.59% and 6.77% respectively. However, the combination of the heaviest two sectors account for over 87.6% of the fund's total assets, so it's safe to assume these will have the largest impact on the daily price movement and total return potential.

The fund has a total of 40 holdings and at first glance I can see a mix of Mortgage REITs and BDCs (Business Development Companies). Invesco Mortgage Capital (IVR) is the largest holding at 4.5%. This is followed by Orchid Island Capital (ORC) and Two Harbors Investment (TWO), which both operate as mortgage REITs. I also see some BDCs that I consider to be less favorable, such as Trinity Capital (TRIN) and BlackRock TCP Capital (TCPC), which are two funds that I previously covered:

  • Trinity Capital: Q4 Earnings Reveal Improved Fundamentals - Hold Rating
  • BlackRock TCP Capital: Q4 Earnings Show No Signs Of Improvement - Sell Rating

Despite my subpar ratings on the Business Development Companies within KBWD, I actually believe that these are some of the more reliable holdings within the ETF. Unlike Mortgage REITs, they do a better job at preserving investor capital over time.

I tend to stay away from Mortgage REITs because they are infamously known to erode investor capital over time. For instance, look at the price changes of some of the top holdings within KBWD. Not a single one of these Mortgage REITs have seen a positive price return and every single one of them have decreased by more than 60% over the last decade, with IVR falling as much as 96.8%. Another trait of these Mortgage REITs is that they tend to see falling dividend payouts over time as well.

This is the nature of Mortgage REITs as they are very sensitive to the interest rate environment, which don't make them good long term holds. So despite KBWD offering an ETF structure, I do not believe KBWD to be a long term hold either. Instead, this sort of ETF is best used as a vehicle to trade in and out of over time.

Risks & Outlook

Since the majority of the underlying holdings within KBWD are within the Mortgage REIT and financial space, the fund has a natural sensitivity to higher interest rates. Looking at the chart below, we can see how KBWD has an inverse relationship with the federal funds rate. When interest rates were cut to near zero levels following the pandemic, KBWD's price rapidly increased. A lower cost of borrowing meant that Mortgage REITs were able to thrive in a more favorable net interest spread environment.

Conversely, when interest rates were rapidly hiked throughout 2022 and 2023, we can see how KBWD's price rapidly fell to the downside. A higher cost of borrowing led to lower interest spreads, which reduced profits, book value per share, and dividend coverage for Mortgage REITs. As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, conditions are still unfavorable for KBWD. While future interest rate cuts can positively serve as a catalyst for growth, the outlook around when interest rate cuts will take place is still questionable.

Due to stubbornly high inflationary data, the Fed has chosen to keep rates unchanged for now. The Fed only estimates there to be one interest rate cut to take place over 2025 since inflation remains higher than anticipated. Depending on which direction inflation goes, I believe there's even a chance that we see interest rates get increased over 2025, which could further suppress the price and overall appeal of KBWD.

An additional risk would be the fund's poor track record of generating a sufficient level of earnings. Unfortunately, the latest reporting is outdated and is from February of 2024. However, we can use it as a reference point to see that KBWD's NAV has consistently decreased over time and still remains below its pre-pandemic level. For instance, here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $19.81
  • 2020: $13.75
  • 2021: $20.68
  • 2022: $17.20
  • 2023: $15.99
  • 2024 (semi-annual): $15.08

With the exception of 2021, KBWD has also reported consistently net realized losses. While net investment income levels have remained fairly consistently, the continued losses have prevented the fund from generating a sufficient amount of earnings to improve the NAV growth and distribution coverage. I just don't see there being a catalyst for growth within the next twelve months. Even if interest rates are reduced, we don't necessarily know how small or large of a cut will be implemented.

Looking at the fund's performance since inception, the price has declined by more than 45%. However, the continuous dividend payouts have resulted in a total return over 105%. While the total return is ultimately positive, investors need to determine whether it's worth the severe deterioration of your initial invested capital, assuming the price decline continues. Perhaps KBWD should implement a sizeable distribution cut as a way to help preserve the NAV until conditions improve.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1423 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.8%. If we multiply the current payout by twelve, we get an estimated annual distribution amount of $1.7076 per share. Referring back to the same report above, the only time that KBWD has generated a sufficient amount of earnings to support this distribution amount was back in 2021. The continued weak dividend coverage has ultimately led to a slow decline of the payouts over time. For this reason, I wouldn't suggest KBWD for investors looking to create a reliable and growing stream of supplemental dividend income.

Additionally, it's worth noting that the distributions received from KBWD are usually funded by net investment income. This means that payouts are classified as ordinary dividends, which have the least favorable tax consequences when compared to the qualified dividends you'd receive from traditional dividend paying companies. Based on the amount of capital invested and your specific tax bracket, a position in KBWD would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Even when running back test of an original investment of $25,000 at the start of 2016, we can see how the annual dividend income received would have decreased over time. The graph below assumes that no additional capital was ever invested after the initial $25k and that no dividends were reinvested. This is an assumption that represents an investor that wants to live off the dividend income received.

2016 Dividend Income: $2,374

2024 Dividend Income: $2,174

Takeaway

For these reasons, I rate KBWD as a sell in the current market environment. Mortgage REITs continue to perform poorly in a higher interest rate environment. Even though KBWD does include some Business Development Companies within, the ones that are selected aren't known to be the best performers. The fund ultimately suffers from a declining NAV and lower payouts over time. While opportunistic investors can certainly utilize the fund to capture some high yielding income, I wouldn't suggest KBWD as a long term buy and hold ETF because of the continuous poor performance of underlying holdings.

data.title: Protalix BioTherapeutics Is A Strong Buy With Increasing Revenues And A Promising Pipeline
data.description:
Introduction

Protalix BioTherapeutics (PLX) is a biotechnology company with two marketed therapies: Elelyso, for Gaucher disease, and Elfabrio for Fabry Disease. As well, the company is actively developing a clinical pipeline that, in our view, may open up further significant revenue opportunities.

We initiate a Strong Buy rating on PLX. This is based on the company’s stable income, profitability, and the longer-term potential of pipeline programs like PRX-115 and PRX-119. In our opinion, increasing royalties from Elelyso and Elfabrio, combined with the potential for additional value-driving catalysts from the clinical readouts related to PRX-115 and PRX-119, create a favorable risk-reward profile at current price levels.

Stock Performance

PLX shares have traded under $3 for the past 12 months. As of April 2025, shares sit near $2.30. With these positive developments in 2024, including the elimination of convertible debt and achieving profitability, we believe the stock will continue to rise significantly. We think the current stock price underappreciates the platform’s potential and the significance of the company's debt-free status and profitability.

Pipeline and Commercial Developments

Protalix currently markets Elelyso (taliglucerase alfa) for the treatment of Gaucher disease. Elelyso was the first plant cell-expressed drug approved by the FDA. Pfizer (PFE) handles commercialization in various territories under an existing agreement. The therapy contributes to Protalix’s revenue stream and was a key factor in the company turning profitable in 2024. While exact sales figures were not broken out in recent filings, the company has indicated they expect royalties from this product to increase through 2030.

Elfabrio (pegunigalsidase alfa) was FDA and EMA approved in May 2023 for the treatment of adult patients with Fabry disease. This PEGylated enzyme replacement therapy was developed through a partnership with Chiesi Global Rare Diseases, which now manages global commercialization. Elfabrio has already had a material financial impact on Protalix's bottom line. In Q2 2023, Protalix reported $11.7 million from Chiesi and a $20 million milestone following FDA approval. As sales grow, we expect Elfabrio will provide a meaningful stream of non-dilutive funding for future pipeline investments.

PRX-115:

PRX-115 is a PEGylated uricase therapy designed to help patients with uncontrolled gout. In a completed Phase I study, PRX-115 was well-tolerated and achieved durable uric acid reduction. At higher doses, uric acid levels remained below 6.0 mg/dL for up to 12 weeks. A Phase II trial is expected to begin in the second half of 2025.

In our opinion, the key to PRX-115’s success will be how it compares to KRYSTEXXA (pegloticase), which is currently approved for a similar population. KRYSTEXXA is associated with immunogenicity and often requires immunosuppressant co-treatment. If PRX-115 can achieve similar uric acid control with fewer immune complications, the drug could compete very well with KRYSTEXXA. But investors should be aware that this is all speculative as the data are not yet available.

PRX-119:

This is a long-acting DNase I enzyme being developed for disorders related to neutrophil extracellular traps, or NETs. The compound is still in preclinical testing. While NETs are thought to play a role in autoimmune and inflammatory disease, we are aware of no NET-targeted therapies that have received regulatory approval. We think this program could be valuable longer term, but we cannot place a value on it currently because it is still way too early to tell.

Market Opportunity

The market opportunity in the gout indication appears to be large and growing. The market is expected to hit approximately $4.9 billion by 2031. Allopurinol or febuxostat remain the standard of care, and they are typically effective. But these treatments are medically contraindicated for some patients (due to intolerance), and some patients do not respond well to them due to lack of effectiveness.

We think this creates a smaller, yet commercially meaningful segment for Protalix. After all, KRYSTEXXA, marketed by Amgen (AMGN) and targeting this group, brought in $716 million in 2022. In our view, if PRX-115 performs well in further trials, it could capture part of this market. In turn, it could represent a meaningful opportunity for Protalix shareholders.

For PRX-119, however, the commercial picture remains unclear to us. The potential indications are varied, but we are aware of no products that are approved to directly target NETs. We believe the opportunity is real but very speculative.

Elelyso and Elfabrio provide Protalix with two established commercial assets. Though the Gaucher and Fabry disease markets are both relatively small, these therapies offer recurring revenue and demonstrate that the company’s ProCellEx platform can yield clinical and commercial success.

Financial Overview

At year-end 2024, the company reported $34.8 million in cash and equivalents. Importantly, Protalix ended the year with zero debt after repaying its convertible notes in full. Net income for 2024 was $2.9 million, or $0.04 per share. R&D expenses were $13 million, down from $17.1 million for year-end 2023.

While we expect future pipeline developments and marketing efforts will increase spending, the company's recent financial performance gives it flexibility without immediately requiring a capital raise. Notably, Protalix reported positive cash flow from operations in three of the four quarters in 2024, with modest cash burn observed only in one quarter (Q1 was $4.2 million, Q2 -$3.6 million, Q3 $4.1 million, and Q4 $4.0 million).

Although our earlier discussion referenced a $13 million R&D expense as a rough proxy for burn rate, a more complete picture is extracted from examining cash flow from operations. On a full-year basis, Protalix generated positive operating cash flow of approximately $8.7 million, which reflects the contribution of royalty revenues and milestone payments. Based on the $34.8 million cash position at year-end, we believe the company has enough cash to fund its core activities for several years.

That said, if development efforts around PRX-115 or PRX-119 accelerate more quickly than expected, or if commercialization investments expand significantly, the company could require additional capital. We do not think such a raise is imminent based on strong cash flow from operations, but we believe it remains a possibility over the medium term, depending on clinical and commercial progress.

Potential Buyout Scenario

We think a buyout scenario is a reasonable possibility if the company continues to execute. Pfizer already markets Elelyso with Protalix. In our view, if PRX-115 shows promising Phase II results and Elelyso and Elfabrio continue to grow, Pfizer could view Protalix as an acquisition target.

While no discussions have been disclosed, we think Pfizer's relationship with Protalix leaves the door open for a buyout if the latter's platform continues to yield clinical and commercial success.

Near-Term Catalysts

We believe investors should be on the lookout for the following potential near-term catalysts:

  • Initiation of Phase II trial for PRX-115 in H2 2025.

  • Partnership updates or data readouts related to PRX-119.

  • Continued royalty growth from Elelyso driven by Pfizer’s efforts.

  • Global sales updates from Chiesi for Elfabrio.

  • Regulatory or market updates related to NETs as a therapeutic target.

  • Any buyout rumors or strategic announcements from Pfizer.

Risks

We think there are still significant risks investors should consider:

  • High Clinical Risk: PRX-115 has only completed early-stage testing. Phase II data could fall short. Needless to say, if PRX-115 underperforms, the company has no late-stage pipeline fallback.

  • Increasing Cash Requirements: Additional trials will require funding. If sales of Protalix's marketed products do not continue to pick up, we expect dilution is possible down the line.

  • Regulatory and Platform Risk: ProCellEx is not yet a widely accepted platform. There may be regulatory hurdles or manufacturing challenges that could hinder Protalix's clinical and commercial success to date.

Conclusion

We believe Protalix has executed extremely well over the past year by eliminating debt and delivering profitability. With two FDA-approved drugs generating revenue, a pipeline candidate entering mid-stage trials, and a platform that has been validated through a partnership with Pfizer, we think the risk-reward setup for an investment is very favorable.

While significant risks remain, the current valuation does not fully reflect what, we believe, is significant upside. As a result, we are initiating coverage with a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Cumberland Pharmaceuticals Inc. 2024 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation

data.title: Markets Look Past Ice-Cold March CPI, Eyes On Tariffs
data.description:
The March CPI report was ice cold—consumer prices fell by the most in five years. The headline rate increased by just 0.05%, bringing the year-on-year pace to 2.39%, sharply below consensus estimates. The core CPI rate actually ticked down by 0.05% on a month-over-month basis, making for a year-on-year rate of 2.79%. Supercore prices fell in the final month of the first quarter.

Both the annual headline and core inflation rates are now the lowest since early 2021. Of course, this is now dated data in light of President Trump’s tariffs that are already in effect. Bank of America reported that March consumer spending ramped up ahead of the implementation of the increased duties.

Digging into the report, shelter prices jumped 4% in March, and food away from home rose 3.8%. Dragging on March CPI were gasoline, which fell by 9.8%, while new car prices were flat. Recent views of the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index point to modest auto deflation, but that, too, won’t last given the steep auto tariffs that will seep in over the months ahead.

Core good CPI is now down 0.1% YoY, while Housing fell notably to 3.99% from 12 months ago. Key for the Fed, core services ex-housing is now just 2.91%--the softest since early 2021. That figure plunged 24 basis points in March.

A Very Tame March CPI Report

March Headline CPI Down in March, Core CPI Rate Inches Up Less Than Expected

Headline CPI +2.79% YoY, Core CPI +2.39% YoY

US Core CPI YoY: Lowest Since March 2021

Deflation in Supercore CPI

It was a muted market reaction to what was a very encouraging inflation update. S&P 500 futures (SPX) drifted lower, falling from a 1.7% loss to 2.0%. The Treasury market caught a bid, though. The yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) fell a few basis points but then drifted back to unchanged on the session, with particularly strong buying on the short end of the curve.

The US dollar (DXY) fell further after the 8:30 a.m. ET data dump, which included sanguine jobless claims figures for last week. The EURUSD (EUR:USD) pair moved up through the $1.11 figure—the highest since last September.

S&P 500 Futures Remained Red Post-CPI

The Dollar Is Now The Weakest Since Q3 2024

Treasury Yields Were Mixed, But Mainly Lower, After the CPI Report

The market now prices in a 16% chance of a May rate cut by the FOMC, and the odds of a June ease are high at 78%. Bigger picture, traders see a 55% chance of a U.S. recession this year, far below where that market traded before President Trump’s Truth Social post Wednesday afternoon. The S&P VIX Index (VIX) hangs high at 37.

Recession Chances Dropped This Week

Looking ahead, inflation data continues rolling in tomorrow. March PPI hits the tape in the premarket, while the first look at April University of Michigan consumer sentiment crosses the wires at 10 a.m. ET. The producer price report should be more impactful, as UMich remains riddled with political influence. Of course, any further announcements from the White House or Truth Social posts from President Trump will move markets, particularly ahead of the weekend.

Next week, the highlight is Retail Sales, along with Q1 earnings reports rolling in. Good Friday is an NYSE holiday, which will be a nice respite for investors given the incredibly volatile macro environment and stock market in the last week-plus.

Data Deck

The Bottom Line

It was a calm March CPI report, but markets paid little attention to it. We’ll likely get a rate cut in June, but all eyes are fixated on trade updates from the POTUS. Next week’s Retail Sales, if strong, should help keep Q1 GDP in the green. Amid high volatility, expect the roller coaster ride to continue.
